name: Kabyle

allophone_rules.notes: Spirantization of /b/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /m/ (e.g., zembil 'basket' → [zəmbil], not *[zəmβil]). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (illustrated there for the Beni Said Riffian dialect; extended here to Kabyle by analogy — see source notes). Corroborated for Kabyle (Chemini) by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022): /nβ/ → [mb].
Spirantization of /d/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/ (e.g., anda 'where' → [anda], not *[anða]). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (Riffian example; extended to Kabyle by analogy).

Spirantization of /t/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/ (e.g., anta 'which one (f.)' → [anta], not *[anθa]; also corpus-attested: anteɣ). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (Riffian example; extended to Kabyle by analogy). Corroborated for Kabyle (Chemini) by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022): /nθ/ → [nt]. SPEAKER NOTE 2026-07-17: anta is a native Kabyle word — 'which one', feminine of anwa 'which one (m.)'. An earlier revision of this note glossed it as 'you' and a later one dismissed the word as Arabic on that basis; the word is native, only the gloss was wrong.
Spirantization of word-final /t/ is blocked after /l/ (e.g., tamellalt 'egg' → [θaməllalt], not *[θaməllaθ]).
Spirantization of /dˤ/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/.
Spirantization of /d/ is blocked after /l/ (e.g., Ldi 'open' → [ldi], not [lði]).
Spirantization of /t/ is blocked after /m/ (e.g., tasemmumt → [tasemmumt], not [tasemmumθ]).

Spirantization of /k/ is blocked after any consonant: /k/ → [ç] only post-vocalically or word-initially (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07). The sonorant cases (after /r, l, n, m/) are corroborated for Chemini Kabyle by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022, RFP Porto): /nç/ → [ŋk]. Geminate contexts included 2026-07-17: corpus evidence (nettkel, lettkal — k after ⟨tt⟩ /tː/) showed the singleton-only list let k wrongly spirantize after geminates.

Spirantization of /ɡ/ is blocked after any consonant EXCEPT /m/: /ɡ/ → [ʝ] post-vocalically, word-initially, and after /m/ — Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) document /mʝ/ → [mʝ] in Chemini Kabyle, the one environment where the categorical post-consonant blocking (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07) does not apply. Sonorant cases after /r, l, n/ corroborated by the same study. Geminate contexts (including mː, by categorical default — the documented exception is singleton /m/ only) included 2026-07-17 for symmetry with the k rule.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop, with palatal shift: /k/ → [ç] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.469, Riffian; Karaoui et al. 2024 list [ç] in the Kabyle fricative inventory). Applies post-vocalically and word-initially; blocked after any consonant (KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_K_AFTER_CONSONANT). The [ʃ] variant reported for Beni Said Riffian (K&S 1997 p.469) does NOT occur in Kabyle (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07) and is not modelled. Geminate ⟨kk⟩ = /kː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax (non-geminate) bilabial stop: /b/ → [β]. Kossmann & Stroomer (1997), Berber Phonology, p.466 (general Berber statement) and p.469 (Riffian illustration). The tense (geminate) counterpart, written ⟨bb⟩ and mapped to /bː/, is a distinct phoneme and is NOT targeted by this rule (p.466: 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants').
Spirantization of the lax dental stop: /d/ → [ð] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466, general Berber; p.469, Riffian illustration). E.g. adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. Geminate ⟨dd⟩ = /dː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax voiceless dental stop: /t/ → [θ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466-469). E.g. tafat 'light' → [θafaθ], tawwurt 'door' → [θawwurθ], tameṭṭut 'woman' → [θametˤːθ]. Geminate ⟨tt⟩ = /tː/ stays a stop. Applies word-initially before ALL vowels, including /i/ — native-speaker verified 2026-07-17: tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal all begin [θi-]; consistent with the 2026-07-16 verification table (tibḥirt, ticcert, tiddi, tigawt). Intervocalic -ti- remains to be verified.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop: /ɡ/ → [ʝ], post-vocalically and word-initially; blocked after any consonant EXCEPT /m/ (KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_G_AFTER_CONSONANT): Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) document /mʝ/ → [mʝ] in Chemini Kabyle. The Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 example argaz → [arʝaz] is Riffian-only; in Kabyle, argaz surfaces with a stop [arɡaz]. Geminate ⟨gg⟩ = /ɡː/ stays a stop in all contexts.
Spirantization of the lax emphatic (pharyngealized) dental stop: /dˤ/ → [ðˤ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468). Geminate ⟨ḍḍ⟩ = /dˤː/ stays a stop.

Vowel backing: /a/ → [ɑ] before emphatic and uvular consonants, singleton or geminate (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463-464: backing/pharyngealization is an automatic, place-conditioned spreading effect, not gemination-sensitive — consistent with KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM's treatment of velar/uvular geminates below). Fixed in this revision: geminate uvulars/emphatics (χː, ɣː, qː, tˤː, etc.) were previously missing, causing words like axxam 'house' to incorrectly surface as [æχːæm] instead of [ɑχːɑm].
Vowel backing: /a/ → [ɑ] after emphatic and uvular consonants, singleton or geminate (see KAB_A_BACKING).
Default realization of /a/ as [æ] when not backed by emphatic/uvular context (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463; Wikipedia).
Vowel lowering: /i/ → [ɪ] in closed syllables (before consonant clusters).
Vowel lowering: /u/ → [ʊ] in closed syllables (before consonant clusters).
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [ŋ] before a dorsal (velar/uvular) consonant.
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [m] before a labial consonant.
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [ɲ] before a palatal consonant /j/.

notes: Kabyle (Taqbaylit), Northern Berber (Amazigh) language of Kabylia, north-eastern Algeria, ~5-7 million speakers (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997, Berber Phonology, p.461). INPUT CONTRACT: this spec expects text in the standardised Berber LATIN alphabet (the tamaziɣt/INALCO orthography descending from Mammeri's conventions, used by the Kabyle Wikipedia, the press, and Naït-Zerrad's teaching grammars). Tifinagh (the neo-Tifinagh abjad-alphabet) is a secondary script for Kabyle and is NOT modelled here; a Tifinagh text must be transliterated to the Latin orthography first. Hallmark grapheme->IPA correspondences: c=/ʃ/, č=/t͡ʃ/, ǧ=/d͡ʒ/, j=/ʒ/, ɣ=/ɣ/ (uvular [ʁ] realisation), x=/χ/, ḥ=/ħ/, ɛ=/ʕ/, q=/q/, and the emphatic (pharyngealized) series ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ = /rˤ sˤ dˤ tˤ zˤ/ (Proto-Berber had only ḍ and ẓ pharyngealized; the rest are Arabic-integrated — Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464). Three phonemic vowels /a i u/ with wide context-dependent realisation (a:[a~ɑ~æ], i:[e~i], u:[o~u]; Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463); ⟨e⟩ writes the epenthetic schwa [ə], which is largely predictable from syllable structure / sonority and mostly non-phonemic (ibid. p.463 §23.4.2). THE SIGNATURE FEATURE is SPIRANTIZATION (modelled as allophone_rules): the lax (non-geminate) stops b d t k g ḍ surface as the fricatives [β ð θ ç ʝ ðˤ], while the tense (geminate) stops — written by doubling the letter (bb dd tt kk gg ḍḍ) and mapped to the distinct long phonemes /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/ — remain stops and are never spirantized (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466: 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants'; the lax:tense opposition is durational — lax consonants are 'always realized shorter than their tense counterparts', p.465). Because the geminates are represented as separate long phonemes, the spirantization rules are naturally gemination-conditioned: they target /b d t k ɡ dˤ/ but not /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/. e.g. igenni 'sky' → [iʝənni], adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. SOURCING CAVEAT: the worked spirantization examples and the homorganic-nasal blocking rule on pp.468-469 are given in the source specifically for the Beni Said Riffian dialect (§23.7.1), not for Kabyle directly — the source's only Kabyle-specific statement is the general remark (§23.5.1, p.466) that spirantization 'reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle.' These rules are extended to Kabyle here by analogy between the two dialects, not from a Kabyle-specific citation. CURRENT REVISION (2026-07-17): (a) REMOVED KAB_SPIRANT_K_ALT and the [ʃ] allophone of /k/ — the k→ʃ value is given in K&S 1997 p.469 for Beni Said Riffian only; per native-speaker judgment it never occurs in Kabyle. (b) /t/ before /i/: a KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_T_BEFORE_I rule was added and then REMOVED on 2026-07-17 after speaker re-verification — word-initial /t/ before /i/ is ALWAYS [θ] (tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal); the 2026-07-16 table stands. Intervocalic -ti- (asiti, itiknikanen, tirmitin) is the residual open item. (c) GENERALIZED k/ɡ blocking to KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_K_AFTER_CONSONANT / KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_G_AFTER_CONSONANT: k → [ç] and ɡ → [ʝ] only post-vocalically and word-initially, stops after any consonant (native-speaker judgment), with the single exception /mʝ/ → [mʝ] from Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022). The sonorant cases (k after r/l/n/m; ɡ after r/l/n) are corroborated by that study (Chemini Kabyle). (d) CORRECTED the Bedar–Quellec–Tifrit reference — the study is real but was miscited (wrong author first names, spurious 'BLS 50' venue and eScholarship link, unverifiable 'four varieties/Table 6' claims); the correct reference is Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022), RFP 2022 Porto, HAL hal-03691265. (e) Fixed broken rule examples (zembil for mb-blocking; snat for nt-blocking), completed the allophones table for all phoneme targets (tˤ, dˤː, tˤː and all geminates), and extended KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM to the geminate uvulars χː/ɣː. Earlier revisions added the geminate graphemes (xx, ww, yy, pp, jj, hh), v→/v/, the vowel-backing rules extended to geminate uvulars/emphatics (axxam → [ɑχːɑm]), palatal nasal assimilation /n/→[ɲ], and the homorganic-nasal plus /l/ and /m/ blocking rules for b/d/t/ḍ. PENDING SPEAKER CHECKS (2026-07-17): intervocalic -ti- ([t] or [θ]? candidates: asiti, itiknikanen, tirmitin); sk-clusters of the askar type — spec yields [sk] categorically, but one 2026-07-16 table entry had [asçar]; if confirmed without an epenthetic schwa, the k rule needs an /s/ refinement. KNOWN LIMITS (documented, not modelled): (1) morphophonological gemination alternations (geminated ɣ surfaces as qq, geminated w as bb, geminated y as gg) are lexical/morphological and are not derivable from the Latin spelling; (2) word-boundary sandhi (n+w → bb/pp, d+t → ts) is not modelled; (3) full vowel allophony (i→e, u→o) is only partially covered; (4) Kabyle stress is largely non-distinctive and predictable, and is not marked here. Labiovelarized kʷ gʷ are usually left unwritten in the standard orthography and are not modelled.

ancestors.notes: Immediate Glottolog subgroup (kaby1244): Afro-Asiatic > Berber > Kabyle-Atlas Berber > Kabyle. Traditionally within Northern Berber.
Arabic adstrate/superstrate: heavy lexical borrowing and the source of most of Kabyle's non-Proto-Berber emphatics and pharyngeals (ḥ /ħ/, ɛ /ʕ/, ṣ /sˤ/, ṭ /tˤ/), per Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464.

sources.author: Kossmann, Maarten G. & Stroomer, Harry J.
sources.title: Berber Phonology
sources.publisher: In A. S. Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa, vol. 1, Eisenbrauns
sources.notes: PRIMARY source, read in full (verified directly, including a re-check of p.469). p.461: Kabyle ~7 million speakers. p.463 §23.4.1: three vowels a i u and their context realisations; §23.4.2 schwa placement by structure/sonority. p.464 §23.4.3.1: pharyngealization described as an automatic spreading feature under the influence of a pharyngealized phoneme; Proto-Berber only ḍ ẓ pharyngealized. p.465 §23.4.3.1.2: lax vs tense opposition, lax always shorter than tense (geminate). p.466 §23.5.1: general Berber statement that spirantization 'reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle' and that 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants.' IMPORTANT CAVEAT: the chapter has NO dedicated Kabyle phonology section — only Tashelhit (§23.6), Riffian (§23.7), and Touareg (§23.8) are treated in detail. The worked spirantization examples and values (b→β, d→ð, g→ʝ/ɣ, k→ʃ/ç) and the homorganic-nasal spirantization-blocking rule (illustrated with mb/nd/nt clusters) all appear on pp.468-469 under §23.7.1, specifically for the Beni Said Riffian dialect, and are extended to Kabyle in this spec by analogy rather than by direct citation. Re-checked p.469 specifically for a claimed /l/,/r/,/n/ blocking rule for /k/,/ɡ/ spirantization: NOT FOUND — the only blocking condition stated there is the homorganic-nasal rule; the generalized post-consonant blocking rules for k/ɡ in this spec therefore rest on native-speaker judgment, corroborated for the sonorant cases by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022), not on K&S. Note: the source does not give an explicit, gemination-specific statement about vowel backing before uvulars/emphatics (the geminate-extension of KAB_A_BACKING/KAB_A_BACKING_PRECEDING rests on phonological reasoning from the p.464 spreading-feature description plus internal consistency with KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM, not a direct textual citation for geminate contexts).

sources.author: Karaoui, Fazia; Djeradi, Amar; Djeradi, Rachida
sources.title: Acoustic Characterization of the Noise Sources for the Kabyle Fricatives Consonants
sources.notes: Read. Confirms the Kabyle fricative inventory [s, f, ʃ, v, ʒ, z, ħ, h, ʕ, ç, ʝ] (p.123 abstract) — the spirantized velars [ç ʝ] are phonemic surface fricatives — and lists spirantization, labio-velarization, palatalization and affrication as Kabyle-specific features (p.123 §1).

sources.author: Bedar, Amazigh; Quellec, Lucie; Tifrit, Ali
sources.title: Post-sonorant occlusivization in Kabyle
sources.publisher: 19th Meeting of the French Phonology Network (RFP 2022), Porto, Portugal, 7-9 June 2022 (conference presentation)
sources.notes: Read. Kabyle-specific study of post-sonorant occlusivization based on Chemini Kabyle (south-east Bejaïa), in an Element-Theory framework. Shows lax (non-strident) fricatives occlusivize after sonorants: /nβ/→[mb], /nθ,nð/→[nt,nd], /nç,nʝ/→[ŋk,ŋg] (with homorganic nasal assimilation), /lθ,lð/→[lt,ld]; crucially /mʝ/→[mʝ] — the voiced dorsal does NOT occlusivize after /m/. Corroborates the sonorant subset of the generalized post-consonant k/ɡ blocking rules in this spec; the generalization itself rests on native-speaker judgment. NOTE: an earlier revision of this spec miscited this study (wrong author first names, 'BLS 50' venue, eScholarship link, 'four varieties/Table 6' claims) — corrected 2026-07-17.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Kabyle language
sources.notes: SECONDARY cross-check (flagged). Used only to confirm the standard 34-letter Berber Latin alphabet grapheme table (c=ʃ, č=t͡ʃ, ǧ=d͡ʒ, j=ʒ, ɣ, x=χ, ḥ=ħ, ɛ=ʕ, q, emphatics ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ, ⟨e⟩=schwa, spirantized b d g k t → β ð ʝ ç θ) against the primary phonology sources. NOT used as the source of any phonological claim.

sources.author: Dallet, Jean-Marie
sources.title: Dictionnaire kabyle-français: parler des At Mangellat, Algérie
sources.notes: Lexical source for the TTS gold sentences.

sources.author: Naït-Zerrad, Kamal
sources.title: Grammaire moderne du kabyle / tajerrumt tatrart n teqbaylit
sources.notes: Grammatical/lexical source for the TTS gold sentences.

sources.author: Chaker, Salem
sources.title: Propositions pour la notation usuelle à base latine du berbère
sources.publisher: Centre de Recherche Berbère – INALCO, Paris (synthèse de l'atelier des 24-25 juin 1996)
sources.notes: Direct source for the alphabet assumed by the INPUT CONTRACT of this spec. Explicitly phonological in inspiration: its own consonant chart lists the lax stop/spirant pairs (b/β, t/θ, d/ð, k/ç, g/ʝ) and prescribes writing tension by letter doubling. This is the 'tamaziɣt/INALCO' notation the spec's grapheme table encodes.

sources.author: Mammeri, Mouloud
sources.title: Précis de grammaire berbère (kabyle)
sources.notes: Codified the Latin orthography for Kabyle on which the INALCO 1996 standard and modern usage build; the conventions assumed by the INPUT CONTRACT descend from this work.

sources.author: Souag, Lameen
sources.title: Kabyle in Arabic Script: A History without Standardisation
sources.publisher: In: Creating Standards, De Gruyter, pp. 273-296. DOI 10.1515/9783110639063-011
sources.notes: Documents that the Latin orthography became dominant for Kabyle only in the second half of the 20th century (Mammeri codification, then INALCO 1996 standardization); supports the script assumptions of the INPUT CONTRACT.

sources.author: Wang, Beini
sources.title: Syllabification and Schwa Epenthesis in Kabyle
sources.publisher: McGill Working Papers in Linguistics 26.1, McGill University
sources.notes: Argues all Kabyle schwas are epenthetic repairs of ill-formed clusters, and that geminates are never broken by epenthesis (e.g. [θaqqraːts] 'bottle') — supports the schwa statement in KNOWN LIMITS and bears on sk-type clusters (the askar question). NOTE: an internal project report miscited this paper as 'Yuan Wang, The Kabyle Syllable, University of Delaware' — corrected here.

sources.author: MOKRAOUI, Athmane (boffire)
sources.title: Native-speaker verification sessions for the Kabyle orthography2ipa spec
sources.notes: Item-by-item verification tables of 2026-07-16/17 covering spirantization of /b d t k ɡ/ in word-initial, post-vocalic and post-consonant positions. Rulings incorporated in this spec: categorical post-consonant blocking for k/ɡ with the /mʝ/ exception; word-initial /t/ → [θ] before ALL vowels including /i/ (tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal); rejection of the [ʃ] allophone of /k/; gloss of anta 'which one (f.)'.

sources.author: MOKRAOUI, Athmane (boffire); corpus analysis run 2026-07-17
sources.title: Kabyle corpus audit for cluster attestation and orthographic contamination
sources.notes: 31,085 samples / 21,260 unique words from boffire/tatoeba-en-kab, boffire/nllb_en_kab, boffire/kabyle-english-TM, boffire/kabyle-english-translatewiki. Blocked clusters richly attested (rk 116, lk 100, nk 77, fk 81, tk 103; rg 87, ng 155, zg 63); exotic clusters (xk, qk, zk, ɣk) unattested. Motivated the geminate-context extension of the k/ɡ blocking rules (nettkel, lettkal — k after ⟨tt⟩). Greek/Cyrillic lookalike scan: 25 hits in kabyle-english-translatewiki (to be cleaned at source); other three datasets clean.
